perturbation.original_text: 10.2.1 All Harpoon Patents existing as of the Effective Date are listed on Schedule 10.2.1 (the "Existing Patents"). To Harpoon's Knowledge, all Existing Patents existing as of the Effective Date are subsisting and, to Harpoon's Knowledge, are not invalid or unenforceable, in whole or in part, are being diligently prosecuted in the applicable patent offices in the Territory in accordance with Applicable Law, and have been filed and maintained properly and correctly in all material aspect and all applicable fees have been paid on or before the due date for payment.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.2.1 All Harpoon Patents existing as of the Effective Date are listed on Schedule 10.2.1 (the "Existing Patents"). To Harpoon's Knowledge, all Existing Patents existing as of the Effective Date are generally subsisting and, to Harpoon's Knowledge, are not believed to be invalid or unenforceable, in whole or in part, are being prosecuted in the applicable patent offices in the Territory, and have been filed and maintained correctly in all aspect and all applicable fees have been paid.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'are being diligently prosecuted in accordance with Applicable Law' with 'are being prosecuted', removing the explicit legal compliance requirement. Additionally, 'have been filed and maintained properly and correctly in all material aspect' is weakened to 'have been filed and maintained correctly in all aspect,' and 'all applicable fees have been paid on or before the due date for payment' is changed to 'all applicable fees have been paid,' removing any deadline-specific obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 35 U.S. Code § 41 - Patent fees; payment; collection
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification, specifically the removal of 'on or before the due date for payment' relating to patent fees, contradicts 35 U.S. Code § 41, which mandates that maintenance fees for patents must be paid within specific timeframes to keep the patent enforceable. The vague language in the modified text creates a loophole, allowing for late payments without explicitly defining consequences, thus conflicting with the strict requirements of the law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 35 U.S. Code § 41 - Patent fees; patent and trademark search systems | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 41 - Patent fees; patent and trademark search systems U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) General Fees.— The Director shall charge the following fees: (1) Filing and basic national fees.— (A) On filing each application for an original patent, except for design, plant, or provisional applications, $330. (B) On filing each application for an original design patent, $220. (C) On filing each application for an original plant patent, $220. (D) On filing each provisional application for an original patent, $220. (E) On filing each application for the reissue of a patent, $330. (F) The basic national fee for each international application filed under the treaty defined in section 351(a) entering the national stage under section 371, $330. (G) In addition, excluding any sequence listing or computer program listing filed in an electronic medium as prescribed by the Director, for any application the specification and drawings of which exceed 100 sheets of paper (or equivalent as prescribed by the Director if filed in an electronic medium), $270 for each additional 50 sheets of paper (or equivalent as prescribed by the Director if filed in an electronic medium) or fraction thereof. (2) Excess claims fees.— (A) In general.— In addition to the fee specified in paragraph (1)— (i) on filing or on presentation at any other time, $220 for each claim in independent form in excess of 3; (ii) on filing or on presentation at any other time, $52 for each claim (whether dependent or independent) in excess of 20; and (iii) for each application containing a multiple dependent claim, $390. (B) Multiple dependent claims.— For the purpose of computing fees under subparagraph (A), a multiple dependent claim referred to in section 112 or any claim dependi
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit requirement for timely patent fee payments, stating only that 'all applicable fees have been paid,' whereas the original text specified 'on or before the due date for payment.' This contradicts the implicit requirement of 35 U.S. Code § 41, which governs patent fees and implies timely payment to maintain patent enforceability. While the provided snippets don't explicitly state the timeframe for payment, the removal of the deadline from the original text introduces ambiguity, creating a loophole for late payments, conflicting with the spirit and practical application of patent fee regulations.

perturbation.original_text: AbbVie shall use Commercially Reasonable Efforts to Develop and obtain Regulatory Approval for [***] Licensed Product for [***] for use in [***] Major Market.
perturbation.changed_text: AbbVie shall consider the use of Commercially Reasonable Efforts to Develop and obtain Regulatory Approval for [***] Licensed Product for [***] for use in [***] Major Market.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'shall use Commercially Reasonable Efforts' with 'shall consider the use of Commercially Reasonable Efforts', the changed text makes the obligation to use Commercially Reasonable Efforts discretionary rather than mandatory. This introduces ambiguity and weakens the legal obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR Part 314 - Applications for FDA Approval to Market a New Drug
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification weakens AbbVie's obligation to obtain regulatory approval, which is a critical aspect of pharmaceutical development. 21 CFR Part 314 outlines the requirements for obtaining FDA approval for new drugs. By making the effort discretionary, the modified text could be interpreted as allowing AbbVie to delay or avoid pursuing regulatory approval, which undermines the intent of these regulations to ensure timely access to safe and effective medications.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates AbbVie to 'use Commercially Reasonable Efforts,' while the modified version changes this to AbbVie 'shall consider the use of Commercially Reasonable Efforts.' This introduces ambiguity and weakens AbbVie's obligation, potentially allowing them to delay or avoid regulatory approval. The Law Explanation suggests that this undermines the intent of regulations like 21 CFR Part 314. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms the existence of 21 CFR Part 314, which pertains to applications for FDA approval, strengthening the relevance of the law explanation. This change makes a previously mandatory action discretionary, which creates an ambiguous legal obligation and a legal contradiction under the definition provided.

perturbation.original_text: 8.1 Pharmacovigilance. Within [***] after the License Option Exercise Closing Date, the Parties shall enter into an agreement to initiate a process for the exchange of adverse event safety data in a mutually agreed format, including postmarketing spontaneous reports received by the Party or its Affiliates in order to (a) with respect to AbbVie, monitor the safety of the Licensed Compound or Licensed Product and to meet reporting requirements with any applicable Regulatory Authority and (b) with respect to Harpoon, permit reasonable access to adverse event safety data for Licensed Compounds or Licensed Products, in each case ((a) and (b)) at AbbVie's expense.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.1 Pharmacovigilance. Within [***] after the License Option Exercise Closing Date, the Parties should consider entering into an agreement to initiate a process for the exchange of adverse event safety data in a mutually agreed format, including postmarketing spontaneous reports received by the Party or its Affiliates in order to (a) with respect to AbbVie, generally monitor the safety of the Licensed Compound or Licensed Product and to consider meeting reporting requirements with any applicable Regulatory Authority and (b) with respect to Harpoon, permit access to some adverse event safety data for Licensed Compounds or Licensed Products, in each case ((a) and (b)) at AbbVie's expense.
perturbation.explanation: This modification weakens the obligation to establish a pharmacovigilance agreement. The phrase 'shall enter into an agreement' is changed to 'should consider entering into an agreement,' making the agreement discretionary. 'to meet reporting requirements with any applicable Regulatory Authority' is softened to 'to consider meeting reporting requirements,' weakening the commitment to compliance. And 'permit reasonable access to adverse event safety data' becomes 'permit access to some adverse event safety data,' which adds an ambiguity of how much data, negating accountability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR § 314.80 - Postmarketing reporting of adverse drug experiences
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text's weakening of safety data exchange and reporting contradicts 21 CFR § 314.80, which mandates strict postmarketing reporting of adverse drug experiences. The ambiguous wording creates a possibility for non-compliance with mandatory safety reporting obligations, and diminishes its responsibility in a critical safety mandate, thereby posing risks to patient safety and regulatory compliance.

Enhanced Content :: FR Reference Title 21 This content is from the eCFR and is authoritative but unoffici
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the commitment to pharmacovigilance, contradicting the intention of postmarketing reporting requirements. The original text uses 'shall enter into an agreement' whereas the modified text uses 'should consider entering into an agreement,' making compliance discretionary rather than mandatory. Additionally, the modification from 'to meet reporting requirements' to 'to consider meeting reporting requirements' further weakens the commitment to regulatory compliance. The change from 'permit reasonable access' to 'permit access to some' safety data introduces ambiguity, which undermines accountability. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly mentions 'Postmarketing reporting of adverse drug experiences,' indicating a regulatory focus on this area, which the modified text undermines.
